# Unsupervised Learning Methods

## Clustering Patient Data: A Comparative Analysis

# 1\. Introduction

Unsupervised learning is a type of machine learning (ML) in which algorithms **discover hidden patterns in data** without labels to learn from.  
Unlike supervised learning, where the data is labeled with a specific category or outcome, unsupervised learning algorithms **are tasked with finding patterns and relationships within the data without prior knowledge of its meaning**, so they must discover these patterns and relationships on their own. [medium.com/@amitvsolutions/the-complete-unsupervised-learning-handbook-ml-algorithms-0bc9d91bc7a8](https://medium.com/@amitvsolutions/the-complete-unsupervised-learning-handbook-ml-algorithms-0bc9d91bc7a8)

This strategy is particularly valuable in scenarios where acquiring ground-truth labels is expensive, time-consuming, or fundamentally impossible. **(Hastie et al., 2009\)**  
**Clustering** is one of the most prominent techniques within unsupervised learning. Its primary objective is to partition a dataset into distinct subgroups (clusters) so that data points within each cluster are highly similar. In contrast, those in different clusters are highly distinct  **(Jain, 2010\)**. In healthcare and medical data science, clustering is frequently used for *patient stratification* and *computational phenotyping*. By grouping patients solely based on physiological measurements, demographic data, and clinical test results, researchers can discover novel disease subtypes or identify high-risk patient profiles before a formal clinical diagnosis is established **(De Benedictis et al., 2024; Hartung et al., 2024\)**.  
There is no single "best" clustering algorithm, as different methods make different mathematical assumptions about the shape and distribution of the data (Bishop, 2006). To comprehensively explore the underlying structures in clinical data, within this assignment, 3 distinct algorithmic approaches are compared:

* **K-Means Clustering:** A centroid-based algorithm that partitions the data into distinct, non-overlapping subgroups by minimizing the intra-cluster variance (MacQueen, 1967).  
* **Gaussian Mixture Models (GMM):** A probabilistic model that assumes all data points are generated from a mixture of a finite number of Gaussian distributions with unknown parameters, allowing for soft assignments and elliptical cluster shapes (Reynolds, 2009).  
* **Hierarchical Clustering:** A connectivity-based approach that builds a tree-like hierarchy of nested clusters (a dendrogram), which is particularly useful for understanding the hierarchical taxonomy of the data without pre-defining the number of clusters.


This report details the application of these three unsupervised learning methods to the UCI Heart Disease dataset. The primary objective is to discover meaningful clinical subgroups based solely on patient measurements, completely blind to their actual cardiovascular disease status. By establishing these clusters and subsequently evaluating them against the hidden diagnostic ground truth, this project aims to evaluate the efficacy, trade-offs, and practical implications of distance-based, probabilistic, and hierarchical clustering in real-world health data science.

# 2\. Dataset Description

The project utilizes the **Heart Disease** dataset from the UCI Machine Learning Repository, specifically the processed.cleveland.data subset (Detrano et al., 1989).

* **Size and Structure:** The dataset comprises 303 patient records, 13 clinical features, and 1 target variable.  
* **Target Variable:** The hidden diagnostic label (num) represents the angiographic disease status, where 0 indicates no presence of disease (\< 50% diameter narrowing) and values 1 through 4 indicate the presence of heart disease (\> 50% diameter narrowing) (Detrano et al., 1989).  
* **Features:** The 13 clinical features cover a mix of categorical, integer, and real data types. These include demographic features (age, sex), symptoms and clinical presentation (chest pain type, exercise-induced angina), resting measurements (resting blood pressure, serum cholesterol, fasting blood sugar, resting ECG), stress test measurements (maximum heart rate, ST depression, ST segment slope), and diagnostic test results (number of major vessels colored by fluoroscopy, thalassemia type).

# 3\. Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)

## 3.1 Clinical context

First, all variables from the studied dataset (processed.cleaveland.data) were understood, to add clinical relevance.  
A summary including their data types and distributional characteristics is provided in Table 1\.

| Table 1: Dataset Dictionary and Distributional Characteristics |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Variable** | **Clinical Description** |
| **age** | Age in years |
| **sex** | Biological sex (1 \= male; 0 \= female) |
| **cp** | Chest pain type. Classified into four categories: (1) typical angina, (2) atypical angina, (3) non-anginal pain, and (4) asymptomatic. Angina is typically a manifestation of myocardial ischemia. |
| **trestbps** | Resting blood pressure (mm Hg) upon hospital admission. Values ≥ 130 mm Hg indicate Hypertension Stage 1 (Whelton et al., 2018). |
| **chol** | Serum cholesterol in mg/dl. Levels ≥ 240 mg/dl are clinically classified as high risk for cardiovascular events (National Cholesterol Education Program, 2002). |
| **fbs** | Fasting blood sugar \> 120 mg/dl (1 \= true; 0 \= false). Used as a baseline indicator for prediabetes or diabetes. |
| **restecg** | Resting electrocardiographic results: (0) normal, (1) ST-T wave abnormality, or (2) probable/definite left ventricular hypertrophy. |
| **thalach** | Maximum heart rate achieved during the treadmill stress test. |
| **exang** | Exercise-induced angina (1 \= yes; 0 \= no). Indicates if physical exertion triggered chest pain. |
| **oldpeak** | ST depression induced by exercise relative to rest. A critical ECG marker for identifying myocardial ischemia. |
| **slope** | Slope of the peak exercise ST segment: (1) upsloping, (2) flat, (3) downsloping. Flat or downsloping profiles are strong indicators of compromised blood flow. |
| **ca** | Number of major vessels (0–3) visibly colored by fluoroscopy, directly indicating structural vascular blockages. |
| **thal** | Thalassemia presence (a blood disorder): (3) normal, (6) fixed defect, (7) reversible defect. Reversible defects indicate areas of the heart with transient poor blood flow. |
| **num** | Angiographic disease status (Target). Indicates \> 50% diameter narrowing in any major vessel (Detrano et al., 1989). |

The cohort has a near-symmetric age distribution centered at 54.4 years. However, there is a notable demographic imbalance regarding biological sex, with males comprising 68% of the records. Beyond demographics, baseline clinical measurements reveal a patient population with elevated cardiovascular risk factors. For instance, the mean resting blood pressure (trestbps) sits at 131.7 mm Hg, placing the average patient within the Hypertension Stage 1 range. Furthermore, serum cholesterol (chol) exhibits a heavily right-skewed distribution; while the mean is borderline high at 246.7 mg/dl, the long right tail indicates a subset of patients with extreme hyperlipidemia, reaching up to 564 mg/dl.

| ![][image1] | ![][image2] |
| :---: | :---: |
| *A* | *B* |
| *![][image3]* | *![][image4]* |
| *C* | *D* |
| *Figure 1: Frequency distributions of key continuous and ordinal variables. The plots highlight the extreme right-skewness present in serum cholesterol (chol (A)), ST depression (oldpeak (B)), and fluoroscopy results (ca (C)), contrasted against the moderately left-skewed maximum heart rate (thalach (D)).* |  |

The median chest pain (cp) classification was non-anginal or completely asymptomatic. When subjected to stress tests, most patients achieved high maximum heart rates (thalach), as reflected by a moderate left skew (-0.534). Conversely, severe ischemic responses were relatively rare but clinically pronounced when present. This is evidenced by the highly right-skewed distributions of exercise-induced ST depression (oldpeak, skewness \= 1.241) and fluoroscopy results (ca, skewness \= 1.174), in which the vast majority of patients had no major vessels visibly narrowed.

## 3.2 Data missingness strategy

Before progressing to feature engineering, the dataset's completeness and viability were evaluated. When addressing missing data, the choice was between **deletion methods** (such as listwise or pairwise deletion) and **imputation techniques** (ranging from simple mean/mode substitution to advanced model-based approaches such as K-Nearest Neighbors). In this cohort, data quality is high, with only six missing entries, all isolated to the ca and thal features. Because these missing values affected less than 2% of the total observations, a listwise deletion strategy was employed. This approach was favored over imputation because it introduces no meaningful loss of statistical power at such a low threshold, and it safely avoids injecting artificial bias that might arise from algorithmically guessing sensitive categorical clinical test results.  
After filtering out incomplete records, the final working dataset consisted of 297 valid observations. Within this refined cohort, the binarized target variable (num) remains moderately balanced, with 54% of patients showing no evidence of disease and 46% having confirmed heart disease. This near-even split provides a stable, unskewed foundation for the subsequent unsupervised clustering tasks.

# 4\. Data Preprocessing and Feature Engineering

To prepare the dataset for distance-based and probabilistic unsupervised algorithms, the variables were systematically transformed to ensure mathematical stability, comparability, and optimal algorithm performance.

## 4.1 Encoding process

The first major transformation involved treating features selectively based on their inherent data types. Because clustering algorithms mathematically evaluate the geometric distance or probabilistic variance between data points, features cannot be left in formats that imply false numerical hierarchies.  
Nominal features (cp, restecg, and thal) were one-hot encoded into distinct binary dimensions. This strategy ensures the algorithms treat different categories (e.g., different types of chest pain) as distinct entities rather than ordered clinical severities.  
Conversely, ordinal features (slope and ca) and continuous features were retained in their numerical format, as their values denote quantifiable clinical gradients. Binary features (sex, fbs, and exang) were preserved on their standard 0/1 scale.

## 4.2 Feature scaling

Following the encoding process, feature scaling was applied to harmonize the dataset. Distance-based algorithms, particularly K-Means and Hierarchical Clustering, are highly sensitive to the scale of the input features. For this study, StandardScaler was selected and applied exclusively to the continuous and ordinal features. Min-Max Normalization was explicitly discarded because, as noted in the exploratory data analysis, features like serum cholesterol (chol) and ST depression (oldpeak) contain high right-skewed outliers. If Min-Max scaling had been applied, these extreme outliers would have compressed the vast majority of normal patient data into a small, indistinguishable numerical band near zero, severely impeding the clustering algorithm's ability to differentiate subtle clinical profiles. Furthermore, binary and one-hot encoded variables were intentionally excluded from this scaling process to avoid distorting their natural probabilistic interpretation.

| Feature Name | Original Data Type | Encoding Strategy | Scaling Applied |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **age** | Continuous | None | StandardScaler |
| **trestbps** | Continuous | None | StandardScaler |
| **chol** | Continuous | None | StandardScaler |
| **thalach** | Continuous | None | StandardScaler |
| **oldpeak** | Continuous | None | StandardScaler |
| **slope** | Ordinal | None | StandardScaler |
| **ca** | Ordinal | None | StandardScaler |
| **sex** | Binary (0/1) | None | None |
| **fbs** | Binary (0/1) | None | None |
| **exang** | Binary (0/1) | None | None |
| **cp** | Nominal | One-Hot Encoding | None |
| **restecg** | Nominal | One-Hot Encoding | None |
| **thal** | Nominal | One-Hot Encoding | None |
| *Table 2: Summary of the preprocessing pipeline. The table details the specific categorical encoding and standardisation strategies applied to each feature based on its inherent data type.* |  |  |  |

## 4.3 Dimensionality Analysis (PCA)

With the feature space numerically stabilized, a PCA was performed on the fully scaled dataset solely for variance analysis.  
The explained variance ratio analysis revealed that the dataset's information is broadly distributed rather than concentrated in just one or two clinical drivers. Specifically, the first two principal components (PC1 and PC2) account for 27.64% and 13.99% of the variance, respectively. To capture a robust mathematical representation of the data (retaining at least 85% of the total variance), 9 principal components are required, with a cumulative explained variance of 88.76%.

| ![][image5] |
| :---: |
| *Figure 3: Per-component explained variance across the principal components. The dashed lines denote the mathematical threshold established for this study, demonstrating that 9 principal components are required to capture at least 85% (88.76%) of the dataset's total variance.* |

## 4.4 Feature Selection

Instead of PCA projection, a supervised hybrid voting strategy was employed to perform explicit feature selection, retaining the clinical meaning of the axes.  
To strictly prevent data leakage, the dataset was first split into training (80%) and test (20%) sets, stratified by the disease status. The StandardScaler was then refitted exclusively on the training data.  
The feature selection process utilized a hybrid majority voting strategy (≥ 2/3 votes) across three distinct, model-agnostic filter methods:

1. **ANOVA F-test (KBest):** Evaluated the linear mean differences between the disease and no-disease groups, selecting features that showed statistical significance (p\<0.05).  
2. **ReliefF:** A distance-based algorithm utilized to capture non-linear interactions between variables.  
3. **Minimum Redundancy Maximum Relevance (mRMR):** Selected the top 10 features that maximized relevance to the target while minimizing mutual redundancy.

This approach eliminated features that consistently contributed to weak predictive power or possessed statistically underpowered rare categories. Specifically, fasting blood sugar (fbs) and serum cholesterol (chol) were dropped, refining the dataset to only the most clinically relevant predictors.  
The final clustering matrix was thus reduced to 297 patients and 15 highly relevant, leak-free features, establishing a robust foundation for the comparative clustering analysis.

**Note: Resolving the Dummy Variable Trap**  
During the initial hot-encoding phase, all columns were intentionally retained (drop\_first=False) to ensure every patient's specific category was explicitly evaluated during feature selection. However, retaining all dummy columns results in perfect multicollinearity, which can lead to errors when applying Gaussian Mixture Models (GMMs).  
However, during feature selection, one category from each of the one-hot-encoded nominal variables was eliminated (dropping cp\_1, thal\_6.0, and restecg\_1). This resolved any potential problem.

# 5\. K-Means Clustering

## 5.1 Algorithm Overview

K-Means is a centroid-based unsupervised learning algorithm that partitions observations into *k* mutually exclusive clusters by minimizing the within-cluster sum of squared Euclidean distances (inertia). Each cluster is represented by a centroid corresponding to the mean position of its assigned observations in feature space (MacQueen, 1967).

The algorithm iteratively performs two steps:

1. **Assignment step:** each observation is assigned to the nearest centroid.  
2. **Update step:** centroids are recomputed as the mean of all assigned points.

This process continues until convergence, typically defined as stabilization of cluster assignments or minimal centroid displacement.

Because K-Means assumes convex and approximately spherical clusters, it provides a strong baseline model for exploring latent structure in standardized clinical datasets where distances between patients are meaningful.

For this study, K-Means was applied to the fully preprocessed clustering matrix derived in Section 4, containing 297 patients and 15 selected clinical features.

## 5.2 Model Configuration

To ensure stability and reproducibility, the following configuration was adopted:

* **Initialisation:** k-means++, which strategically places initial centroids to reduce convergence to poor local minima.  
* **Number of restarts:** n\_init \= 20, selecting the best solution among independent runs.  
* **Random seed:** 42 for reproducibility.  
* **Distance metric:** Euclidean distance (default).

The algorithm was evaluated across multiple values of *k* to determine the optimal number of clusters.

## 5.3 Determining the Optimal Number of Clusters

Selecting an appropriate number of clusters is a central challenge in unsupervised learning. Two complementary evaluation metrics were employed across k=2k \= 2k=2 to k=10k \= 10k=10:

### **Elbow Method (Inertia)**

Inertia measures cluster compactness:

* Lower inertia indicates tighter clusters.  
* However, inertia monotonically decreases as *k* increases.

Therefore, the optimal value is identified at the **inflection point** where additional clusters yield diminishing improvements.

The inertia curve displayed a smooth decline typical of medical datasets with overlapping patient profiles. The largest reduction occurred between k=2k=2k=2 and k=3k=3k=3, after which improvements became marginal, suggesting limited additional structure beyond two groups.

### **Silhouette Score**

The silhouette score evaluates both cohesion and separation:

* Values range from −1 to 1\.  
* Higher values indicate better-defined clusters.

The maximum silhouette score was achieved at:

k=2(Silhouette=0.2025)k \= 2 \\quad (\\text{Silhouette} \= 0.2025)k=2(Silhouette=0.2025)

Although moderate in magnitude, this value is expected in clinical datasets where biological variability produces gradual rather than sharply separated patient groups.

### Optimal Choice of k

Both evaluation methods consistently supported:

k=2\\mathbf{k \= 2}k=2

This choice also aligns with clinical interpretability, reflecting the binary diagnostic framing of cardiovascular disease (presence vs. absence) used earlier in the project.

Increasing *k* would artificially fragment clinically similar patients without meaningful statistical justification.

## 5.4 Final K-Means Model

The final model was fitted using k=2k=2k=2.

| Metric | Value |
| ----- | ----- |
| Inertia | 1868.87 |
| Silhouette Score | 0.2025 |
| Cluster 0 | 164 patients (55.2%) |
| Cluster 1 | 133 patients (44.8%) |

The clusters are relatively balanced, indicating that the algorithm did not collapse into a dominant group - a desirable property for downstream interpretation.

## 5.5 Cluster Visualisation in PCA Space

To facilitate interpretation, cluster assignments were projected onto the two-dimensional PCA representation introduced in Section 4.3.

The visualization reveals that separation occurs primarily along the first principal component (PC1), which captures variation related to cardiac stress response variables.

* **Cluster 1** concentrates on the positive side of PC1, associated with:  
  * higher ST depression,  
  * reduced maximum heart rate,  
  * more adverse exercise ECG patterns.  
* **Cluster 0** occupies the negative side, representing comparatively healthier physiological responses.

The projected cluster centroids confirm that separation occurs mainly along this dominant clinical axis rather than across multiple orthogonal dimensions.

## 5.6 Cluster Centre Interpretation

Cluster centres were analysed in both standardized feature space and original clinical units to enable medical interpretation.

### **Cluster 0 - Lower-Risk Cardiac Profile**

Key characteristics include:

* Higher prevalence of **normal thalassemia results** (thal\_3.0).  
* Predominantly normal resting ECG measurements.  
* Higher achieved maximum heart rate during stress testing.  
* Lower ST depression values.  
* Slightly younger patient population.

Overall, this cluster represents patients exhibiting physiological responses consistent with preserved cardiac function.

### Cluster 1 - Higher-Risk Cardiac Profile

This cluster demonstrates multiple indicators associated with cardiovascular impairment:

* Older average age.  
* Reduced maximum heart rate during exercise.  
* Elevated ST depression (exercise-induced ischemia marker).  
* Slightly higher resting blood pressure.  
* Greater presence of abnormal diagnostic indicators.

These patterns suggest compromised cardiovascular performance and align with clinically recognised markers of coronary artery disease.

### **Cluster Centres in Original Units**

| Feature | Cluster 0 | Cluster 1 | Interpretation |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- |
| Age | ≈51 yrs | ≈57 yrs | Higher-risk group older |
| Thalach | ≈158 bpm | ≈138 bpm | Reduced exercise capacity |
| Oldpeak | ≈0.6 mm | ≈1.8 mm | Greater ischemic response |
| Trestbps | ≈129 mmHg | ≈135 mmHg | Slight BP elevation |

### 

### *The consistency of these differences supports the clinical plausibility of the discovered clusters.*

## 5.7 Interpretation and Clinical Implications

Despite operating entirely without diagnostic labels, K-Means recovered a patient stratification closely aligned with cardiovascular risk profiles. This demonstrates that meaningful clinical structure exists intrinsically within physiological measurements alone.

However, the modest silhouette score highlights an important limitation:

* cardiovascular disease progression is continuous rather than discretely separable,  
* patient profiles overlap substantially,  
* strict geometric partitioning cannot fully capture biological complexity.

Thus, while K-Means provides interpretable baseline segmentation, more flexible clustering models may better capture heterogeneous patient subtypes.

## 5.8 Task 3 Summary

| Item | Result |
| ----- | ----- |
| Algorithm | K-Means |
| Initialisation | k-means++ |
| Restarts | 20 |
| k range tested | 2–10 |
| Optimal k | 2 |
| Inertia | 1868.87 |
| Silhouette Score | 0.2025 |
| Cluster sizes | 164 vs 133 patients |
| Main separation axis | Cardiac stress response (PC1) |
| Clinical interpretation | Low-risk vs high-risk patient profiles |
